transcript.text: What part of London are you in?
I'm currently in the sunny south part of London.
This meeting is being recorded.
Apostolos is gracing the office's presence in Hammersmith.
Hey guys.
Awesome.
Yeah.
How did you pronounce your name, by the way?
George said it too quick for me to catch.
Just Apostolos is fine.
It's a long Greek name.
Apostolos is fine.
It definitely looks Greek.
Apostolos, that sounds great.
And George, how close are you to like, you said south London, how close are you to Peckham?
I actually went to like a random like, you know, meet like DJ set in Peckham one night and everyone that was in London was like, why the hell did you go to Peckham?
But yeah, I'm probably like, what, 25 minutes, 30 minutes from Peckham.
So fairly close.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Peckham's got a pretty decent scene like that.
So that would make sense.
Yeah.
Like Bradley Zero.
I'm in Houston, Texas myself.
Been to London a couple times.
And yeah, last time I tried to do some non-traditional tourist events.
That and then Shakespeare Globe was doing a showing of, what's that?
It wasn't a Shakespeare thing.
was like the movie about the witch or the thing about the witches where like everybody's getting accused of witchcraft or something.
I don't know.
I forget the name for some reason.
Anyways, I digress.
You came here to talk to me about growthx and that's what I can talk more in detail about.
I'd love just like a little bit more context on how you heard of us, what you're looking for.
And I'll be sure to make sure you leave here with a good understanding of how we might be able to partner.
Yeah, sure thing.
So I think, well, I personally heard of you guys through LinkedIn.
I see a lot of your sort of organic content and sort of success stories.
So that's obviously prompted a decent reason to reach out, I think.

In terms of what we're looking for, look, I think what we're trying to understand is ways of scaling content, scaling awareness, scaling demand rapidly, and we're starting to sort of understand what options are available to us.

We can obviously build some systems ourselves, that will take time, we're not experts, there's tools that we could use, there's sort of a, maybe the air ops, you know, those types of tools that you can start to use, they take time, they're costly.

So I think we're trying to understand, A, what it is that GrowthX does do, how you do it, and then sort of results that you would, you know, be fairly confident in saying that a business like Causalens could expect from an engagement.
Absolutely, that makes perfect sense.
Yes, Tyler, to give you the, to give you my perspective, we're working.
With roughly like a million of marketing budget, that's without the potential fundraiser we're exploring.
So, and then George and I have a board meeting we need to prepare for like end of November.
So we're actively exploring, like exploring various options of how to best utilize that $1 million budget to help us be scalable.
Makes sense.
So it sounds like you're hoping to determine the path forward, basically by that board meeting or shortly after, whether it's tools, whether it's a partner, and then begin in Q1, like 2026.
Is that accurate or is the timeline faster?
I mean, we can start ASAP, right?
Depending obviously on the commercial model.
But my point is like, we have a, George and I are figuring out how to best allocate that budget, running a lot of experiments these weeks.
And then we need to present a board that, yeah, yeah, is...
Is GrowthX part of the plan for 2026?
If yes, this is how, what we can do with them.
Makes perfect sense.

To give you the high-level context, so we can just really orient to what we do here at GrowthX, think of us as an end-to-end partner with an internal platform that allows us to essentially perform our services at scale, right?
Create quality content for SEO, GEO at scale.
So we'll do everything from SEO and GEO strategy to creating articles that we'll publish daily, and we can even stage and publish in your CMS.
And we'll also do all of the LLM visibility and analytics.
And so typically what we're doing is, you know, like if you work with an SEO agency, we can replace that SEO agency and do a lot more in terms of the breadth of our service.
And then a lot of our clients, they'll leverage, right, what we do from an analytics and reporting side instead of using tools like Scrunch or Profound.
So it's kind of like an all-in.
All-in-one partner, but the work streams that we're really focused on would be editorial content and programmatic content.
So content that lives on your marketing site.
Any just quick clarification questions will obviously go much deeper, but I just wanted to give you that initial context.
That makes sense to me.
Perfect.
The question I have for you is, how do you create that kind of content today?
Do you have like dedicated in-house resources, freelancers, agency partners?
We are a small but mighty team.
We do everything manually at the moment.
So we have a series of topics that we are like, okay, let's go create content.
And that's it, essentially.
We did have an inbound agency that were very traditional, very slow, not sort of wanting to go at the kind of scale and pace that we want to.
Hence kind of why I've reached out to you guys, because I know...
And that's the We're what's
That's that's sort of core fundamental of yours.
But from my perspective, at the moment, we do content manually, blogs, anything like that.
I'm keen to get a bit of a content flywheel going that can be scalable, but also quality, because what I'm quite conscious of is, is, you know, the classic AI slop coming into play.
And I just want to make sure that, you know, I'm all for quantity, and quantity is good, but quality is as fundamental.
Okay, perfect.
And Tyler, for context, just to cover the basics, we do pretty large B2B enterprise sales, right?
We're not a PLG company.
So, of course, content and all of what you mentioned helps with the branding aspect.
That's cool.
But primarily, right, our CEO and the salespeople just need quality.
You know, pretty top-down execs, right?
Yeah, so I think, Apostolos, just to give context, I think what this would be is content that helps us surface for natural searching, for appearing in LLMs, for creating brand awareness.
You know, it's that type of content.
And the sales team could also utilize some of that content for their calls, their follow-ups.
You know, we could have the design agency create a nice, fancy version in a downloadable PDF of something that works.

But I think, for me, this is more about getting as many eyeballs, relevant eyeballs as possible, on the brand, and not relying on people to sort of hit our website and scroll through a thousand blogs and say, okay, this is something interesting, I'll click in and read it.
Yeah.
Exactly.
You know, focus would be, like, you know, really thinking through first, like...
Who's your ICP?
And what are the topics that you want to have authority in, right?
What are the questions that you want to be the answer for?
And the result is like driving higher intent organic traffic, seeing that all the way through to the conversion event.
And so that's the way we think about monitoring the performance of our content and what our ultimate goal would be.
Tell me just a little bit, doesn't have to be too much, like who is your ICP, would you say?
Like who is the buyer, who is the person that you would want to be creating content for?
I can give a quick overview.
So we recently sort of repositioned.
We were previously a sort of deep tech AI lab where we did something called causal AI.
And we were fairly defined in our ICP there, which was quite technical people, quite heavy, you know, heads of data science, those types of personas.
We've moved to what we're calling reliable digital workers.
And primarily the end buyer is someone like.
A COO, someone like a CTO, but ultimately, you know, we want to be supporting the rest of the people within those companies.
So we're also looking at, you know, VP ops, heads of specific departments that manage operational processes as well.
We, because we've just kind of repositioned, we're not entirely golden on the ICP, let's say.
We've got a bit of a focus this quarter on Pharma, and within Pharma, CROs, so contract research organizations, and CTOs, contract commercial organizations.
They all kind of support the Pharma industry.
And what we're really trying to work out is, you know, repeatable job titles, repeatable use cases that we can just sort of pour fuel on.
Fire and just go, go, go.
So, yeah, it's not a consistent, cohesive answer.
Apologies, but that gives you a bit of context, hopefully.
No, I think, you know, sometimes, right, Seed, Series A, they have no idea, like, who their ICP is.
So I love that that's evolved and that you're, you know, continuing to think about that and hone in, right, make sure that it's accurate.
And we can help with that experimentation a ton, right, because we would be creating content for that audience.
And that might shift over time if you're not seeing, right, like, the type of personas converting, right, that are going to help your sales team be successful.
Who would you say are, like, the two, three biggest competitors that you're up against right now?
Because we'll do some, like, analysis on, like, the actual search opportunity against the competitors.
I'm probably better at answering that one.
There are no direct...
direct...
Competitors are in exactly the same positioning.
We're closer, I guess, to, we're doing an outcomes-based model.
So the consultancies are closest to us.
So we're not selling, we're not licensing technology.
We deliver outcomes.
So it's like four deployed model plus our technology.
So typically the consultancies, implementation folks would be closer to us.
So for context, right, you know, there are like a million agentic platforms, a million ways to build agents these days.
We're not competing in that space where we license to tech people, another piece of technology.
We go to business folks and we say, hey, let us solve your compliance process.
Let's automate your regulatory filings.
How?
You get the engineer and you get the technology.
Okay, interesting.

You essentially like create the digital workers for the enterprises and it's, you you've got like some standard types, but it's almost, it's a little bit custom from client to client depending on how.
You could argue we're competing with OpenAI is like 10 million consulting model, right?
So OpenAI would also go and say, hey, we have some premium consulting where you get all of our models, plus you get the engineer.
With that side of OpenAI, yes, we're kind of competing.
Or we're also closer to a Palantir model as well, right?
Where the guys will have the forward deployed people.
Makes a lot of sense.
Thanks for the context.
Let me go through a quick presentation.

I'll go through some examples and I'll get under the hood of the workflows so that you can get a sense of, right, how we ensure that it's not AI slop because, yeah, we're definitely very wary of that.
So as you know, right, like every marketing site is getting disrupted by AI search, but there's a huge opportunity that comes with that.
Clicks are disappearing, but the AI search visits are showing much more likelihood to convert.
SEMrush said like 4.4 times more likely.
They spend three times as long.
And so what's positioned GrowthX really well here is that.
Our founder and CEO has spent his entire career building organic content engines.
He's never really spent much at all on paid.
And like at every stop, you know, there's like a story to the content engine he created.
At IBM, it was this website, securityintelligence.com, that was their highest ROI marketing channel.
And it was live for like over a decade.
And they brought it back into the parent site.
At HashiCorp, he was the marketing leader from six to 100 million.
At scale, he was the CMO into the acquisition by Meta.

And what he did at DeepGram was he replicated that process of building a content engine, typically hiring a big team, and tried to use AI workflows to develop a really robust brief on the assignment, do deep research against that brief, figure out, right, how you can actually draft content and then fact check that content.
And our model here is, you know, strategy, AI workflows, but also like experts in the loop, right?
We don't.
People think that just the workflows is going to get the job done.

There's a ton of, just like with your business, there's a ton of strategy that goes in up front to make sure that we develop these artifacts around your ICP or personas, your company and your solution, and then your brand guidelines or voice so that there's the context necessary to make the workflows hyper relevant, right, to the kind of quality that you're trying to scale.
And so part of the way that we do that, and sorry, I have so many meetings, so I'm probably going to just be a little scatterbrained and jump around.
But part of the way we do that is we make sure our workflows go through all the most important factors for GEO.
So we have Deep Researcher, and we do that across several tools.
So like Exa and Tably, right, for deep web research, Perplexity Deep Research, Claude, ChatGPT.
And we make sure that we can customize our workflow so that, like, maybe you just want to make sure that we do research against.
Certain sources over others.
And we're able to essentially create content that's very credible, comprehensive, and then current.
And so refreshing the content is also a part of our service.
And we would do that every three months or so.
This is all powered by our internal platform, which we are rapidly in the process of bringing clients into.
Today, it's just our internal team who uses the platform.
And the idea is that for every client, they get a managing director, a managing editor, and a content manager who all have backgrounds in SEO, content marketing, or journalism.
And so just as like a quick example, our chief content officer was the editor-in-chief for TechCrunch for over a decade.
We've got folks who have built out the G2.com website and ran SEO for TripAdvisor and Fanatics.
And so, you know, it really is a best of both worlds with machines and humans.
And that's why a lot of the clients that you see here.
They're highly technical, right?
Surge AI, augment code, abnormal security, ramp, web flow.
And that's just a little bit around the way that we work.

The way we set it up in terms of the partnership is the first eight weeks is about developing those really rich artifacts and then calibrating on quality so that we can publish daily in the 12-month growth execution.
And so what's great about our partnership is that we make phase one very low risk.
You get the artifacts, you get content, and you have the ability to opt out if you're not feeling confident about the approach that we would scale to in phase two.
But you understand exactly what that would look like, right?
It's basically just, you know, doing what we get to by the last week of phase one, which is publishing these high-quality articles, and then scaling against the roadmap that you've reviewed with us.
On the talking for me, any questions before I jump into some examples and the platform itself?
Thank you.
Thank you.
Nope, that's good to me.
Cool.
Cool.
So thinking about some different content we've created for our customers, you know, a lot of editorial is going to be blog in nature, but we can think outside of the blog, right?
For augment code, we created like 100 plus guides over the last three, four months, and they wanted us to do that for a couple of reasons.
One, they wanted this to be more like thought leadership and comparing different tools in the AI coding space.
Two, they wanted to compare it against the blog that they were working on internally, and we've like two to three X the traffic on their blog, so we've done a pretty good job there.
When you think about like blog content, know, Town is a seed stage company, and they chose us as a partner because accuracy was like the most important part of the content that they were creating.
You can't give bad tax advice.
And so you can see here, like if we look at this piece of content.
We do a lot of cross-linking, but we can also bring your voice in.
We had a bunch of quotes from their team, and they were like, find the best articles to insert some of these quotes.
And so we've got like their natural voice coming through the content so that it's not just, right, a bunch of very technical details around certain tax scenarios.
And so we definitely have been able to scale quality in a variety of ways for our customers, whether it's more, you know, thought leadership in their space with AI.
Or whether it's, you know, creating like this glossary of SEO-optimized terms that, you know, Abnormal wants to be able to help people better understand.
And so like, in short, where I go into the platform, we're audience first.

We really think through what are all the questions that your ICP might have, and how can we create the best answers to those questions systematically so that we can give you the best possible chance, right?
Because when you're domain-
Authority is lower when you're just starting out, it takes longer, but we can basically do everything in the way that optimizes for visibility.

And whether it's Augment Code, right, who joined us at around 20K monthly traffic, and, you now we're over 2x that in a matter of three or four months, or whether it's Tiro, who, you know, they barely had a blog set up, and we've over 6x their traffic, you know, they're a little more niche, right?
Like it's dental staffing software, so it's pretty, pretty easy to shine against the competition there.
But yeah, I mean, we've seen like a variety of use cases, and when you look at Ramp, this is a cool programmatic example.
We've created this entire vendor directory for them, and it's populating each vendor page with their own internal spend data, right?
And for Ramp, like they, and we designed everything that you see here in terms of the charts, and for Ramp,
They want to be the authority around any kind of business spending.
And so in this vendor database, it's tool reviews, but it's also what are the alternatives, right?
And what about this tool versus another tool?
And then you can also see that we've created like a per diem calculator where we've got a page for every city in the U.S.
And there's like a config around setting dates, right?
And actually seeing like what is the average that people spend in that city?
And then even like a business expense classifier.
And so it's like everything associated with business spending is the content that we've created for them on their blog, but also programmatically.
So these are just like a couple of examples.
Tyler, that's cool.
How long did it take you to build this for AMP?
We've been working with Ramp for over a year and I've been here for like two months.
So I'm not sure, you know, programmatic upfront takes some time just to make sure we've got all the right inputs to create relevant outputs.
But when it comes to like editorial, we publish daily.
So.
You could imagine that if you're working with us, you'd have content published every business day of the week.
Can you please go back to the commercial model, the slide that you have?
Absolutely, yeah.
And so, you know, 40K is like, or harder to coordinate with.
30K would be, you know, fast-growing startups like you guys.
15K per month on the growth execution would get you the five publish-ready articles per week.
And we can swap out editorial for programmatic during phase two.
But if we wanted to do both in tandem, then you would be looking at adding, you know, 9 to 15K, depending on complexity, to the per-month pricing.
Got it.
And we need to go through.
Phase one, in order to go to phase two?
Phase one is not actually?

Yeah, phase one is really important because if we can't create rich artifacts, if we can't align on what quality looks like, if we can't agree on the topic clusters that we're going to create content assignments around and the prioritization of those topics, then we can't scale to daily publishing.

And if you don't have the opportunity to see the quality that we can get to and align on whether that's meeting your standard for the next 12 months, then there's no point in jumping right into phase two.
I'm coming from a perspective, like, could we not give you all of this ourselves?
So George and I go into a dark room, we sit there for a few days, we prepare all of the deliverables that you have on phase one, and then we say, okay, guys, are we good to launch?
And then I point to, like, what I'm hearing, like, eight.
Twix, just to align.
Sounds like a lot.
Yeah, that's fair.
And I'll go into more detail here so that you can get a better idea of what's actually happening.
So we're going to do our own deep research and SEO audit prior to kicking off.
We're going to be doing that before the eight weeks start.
But then we're going to have a kickoff call with your team.
And keep in mind, we're trying to become experts on your business, your solution, and your brand.
And so a lot of times, especially at an earlier stage, you don't have resources that can help us fully get to that level.
As George mentioned, your ICP has changed.
And so getting into the nuance is really important.
And then we're going to do competitive research.
We're going to keep track of everything.
Like we go really deep with you during the strategy sprint.
It's not like we're just kind of hanging out for
You know, we're developing these artifacts that's going to make the content hyper-relevant.
And so you mentioned, like, a different industry that you're focused on right now, right, like pharma in particular.
With Engine, who's in the business travel space, they're one of our clients, you we created artifacts for them across all of their target industry verticals.
So now they can create hyper-relevant content that's industry-specific.

If you look into, right, like this artifact here, you know, we're understanding everything around their fears, their motivations, how to win, lose their trust, what's going to get them promoted, fired, why they choose, you know, you in particular.
This is kind of like an evolving conversation, not necessarily something that we can just ingest resources and create.

And so the reason we collaborate so deeply in phase one is so that phase two, your only job is really just to review our drafts, right, and give us a thumbs up or even do, like, the last mile execution.
So, like, we prefer.
Or to, you know, have access to be able to stage and publish in your CMS because it allows us to move faster.
But nothing that we stage and publish is going to be done without your approval.
And so that's like a little bit of like what's going into the first part of the engagement in terms of like the content assignments.
We started working with Lovable recently and we've got like the permission to share all of this with you.
And so, you know, with Lovable, we've got these different topic clusters.

We did a bunch, this can take forever to load, but we did a bunch of keyword research around the opportunities, around what the competitor content would be in particular here that we're trying to mimic.
And then for the assignments themselves, you know, we've got like a backlog for them to consider.
And this is something that would be prepared for you in the strategy sprint, right?
That you're able to see, okay, what exactly would GrowthX be working on?
What's the total search volume?
And what's the keywords?
What's the difficulty to rank?
And then what's the competitor content that we would be creating that to be superior to?
Yeah.
And Tyler, help me.
What are the KPIs you would be tracking to see to know if we're doing well?
Yeah, definitely.
So the KPIs that we would be tracking, we set up the Looker dashboard for all of our clients.
We would definitely love to get access to your GSC, Google Search Console, Google Analytics.
But we're really just moving from, you know, zero to four here, essentially, and then keeping dashboards across a variety of metrics that are connected to the KPIs.
But the goal is, you know, you can connect what we're doing to increase AI visibility, increase organic traffic, and then increase conversions that are connected to the content that we're creating.
Sometimes attribution is hard, though, right?
And so...
Right.

We can do that to the extent that, you know, you've set some of it up and we can do our best to build upon that, but, you know, a lot of times we're piggybacking off of what's in your Google Analytics.
Got it.
Are you tracking, I guess, how will we do in GEO?
Oh, absolutely.
Yeah, so we're going to be looking at, we use a partner called Scrunch, and then we're building, like, our own solution here.

But in Scrunch, can see everything from the specific prompts, can see it against any of the different LLMs, you can have those prompts within topics, and we would track, like, your performance week over week, so that you can see, like, where you're showing up, and what position are you showing up.
And we would go very detailed on the LLM side, also.
Okay.
Cool.
Conscious of time, as well.
I need to drop, but, George, yeah, I asked a lot of stuff, I'm
I've got a quick question.
obviously, I mentioned we are sort of honing in this quarter on Pharma, CRO, CCO.

Should that honing in not prove successful and perhaps we go through a process of aligning and creating artifacts on Pharma and CRO, how would we go about potentially expanding or pivoting some of those artifacts in Q1, Q2 next year?
Yeah, we definitely love clients who are like willing to be more experimental, you know, like it's easier to deliver results when you really understand like exactly who you're going after.
But we can evolve artifacts, we can create new artifacts.
It's not something that is like done when we get to phase two or when we start producing content.
So we would just like want to collaborate with you there.
I do apologize.
I have to jump to another call.
I definitely should have used time a little bit better here.
But if you're open.
Into it, George, and Apostolos, we can do a demo as the next step where you can really start to see like the how that I didn't get to show too much.
Thanks, Tyler.
Yeah, I'm happy to.
Happy to stay on the call, Tyler, if you want to quickly arrange something.
Yeah, let's do it.
Let me know what time works best for you, London time.
What time is it now, London time?
Oh, okay.
So I'm in Texas, so we can go a little earlier than a Pacific Coast would do.
Yeah, next week, I can do pretty much any day at between 3 and 5, if that's good for you.
Yeah, Tuesday, 3 o'clock works for me.
Tuesday, 3 o'clock, so it's 5 o'clock now, 3 o'clock is two hours earlier.
Perfect.
I'll shoot you an invite for Tuesday, October 14th at 3 p.m.
London time.
Sounds good.
Appreciate it.
Good to meet you.
Talk soon.
soon.
Good to meet meet
Looking forward to continuing the conversation.
Good stuff.
Thanks, Tyler.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introduce GrowthX's content marketing services to causaLens and explore potential partnership.](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=90.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [causaLens seeks scalable content solutions to drive awareness and demand, with a $1M marketing budget](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=120.0)
  - [GrowthX offers end-to-end content creation, SEO/GEO strategy, and analytics, publishing daily high-quality articles](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=248.0)
  - [Two-phase engagement: 8-week strategy sprint ($80K) followed by 12-month execution ($30K/mo for 5 articles/week)](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=970.0)
  - [causaLens recently repositioned as "reliable digital workers," targeting COOs/CTOs in pharma/CROs](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=509.0)

## Topics

### causaLens Background and Needs

  - [Recently repositioned from deep tech AI lab to "reliable digital workers"](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=509.0)
  - [Target buyers: COOs, CTOs, VP Ops, heads of departments managing operational processes](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=531.0)
  - [Current focus on pharma industry, particularly CROs (Contract Research Organizations) and CCOs](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=561.0)
  - [$1M marketing budget, preparing for board meeting end of November](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=176.0)
  - [Seeking scalable, high-quality content solutions to drive awareness and demand](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=120.0)

### GrowthX Services Overview

  - [End-to-end partner with internal platform for scaled content creation](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=248.0)
  - [Services: SEO/GEO strategy, daily article publishing, LLM visibility, analytics](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=248.0)
  - [Can replace traditional SEO agencies and analytics tools (e.g., Scrunch, Profound)](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=279.0)
  - [Focus on editorial and programmatic content for marketing sites](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [Ensures quality through AI workflows and expert human oversight](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=810.0)

### GrowthX Engagement Model

  - [Phase 1 (Strategy Sprint): 8 weeks, $80K](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=970.0)
      - [Develop rich artifacts (ICP, company/solution details, brand guidelines)](https://fathom.video/share/cb4psRSbnnwE9cfQp69h7LyvTWmPwrUv?tab=summary&timestamp=1388.0)
